extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control hardware. It comprises a lower and upper chassis together with autonomy elements, equipped with a LiDAR sensor that provides point‑cloud data, a motor for propulsion, and a steering servo. The vehicle runs a resource‑constrained electronic control unit (ECU) that executes basic functions but lacks sufficient compute for the full Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) algorithm, making it an ideal candidate for off‑loading computation in a digital‑twin setting.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin system provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a UI shows real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and object tracks; it also allows users to view, save, and generate reports of collected data. (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed computation) is executed on the DT and the resulting speed command is sent back to the vehicle for actuation. (3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set a desired speed, or issue an emergency‑brake command through a control‑panel UI. All services target both operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting via data collection).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture and uses ROS 2 packages for each functional entity (FE). Communication among SW components occurs over a ROS 2 network, while inter‑entity messaging uses Wi‑Fi UDP with <100 ms clock‑synchronisation accuracy. Key enablers include a Data Translation FE for serialization, unit conversion and message‑type alignment; functional entities for Data Collection, Device Control, Data Pre‑Processing (LiDAR clustering & tracking), Actuation, Reporting, Presentation, and a User Interface FE split into 3D visualization and control‑panel components. The system is hosted as an open‑source prototype at the McMaster Centre for Software Certification (McSCert) and leverages standard ISO 23247 entities (Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) together with a custom Physical Twin Entity representing the vehicle’s sensors and actuators.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The ACC digital twin requires the physical and digital components to share a common notion of time with clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms, as imposed by hardware limits and safety‑critical vehicular standards. All services (data monitoring, computation offloading, remote management) operate in real‑time; the system must provide low end‑to‑end latency communication between the vehicle and the DT, though the exact latency bound is not quantified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DTS is built as a single logical twin organized into three ISO‑23247‑derived entities – Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity and User Entity – each deployed on separate hardware. Within each entity a set of Functional Entities (FEs) such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Data Translation, Reporting, Visualization, and Controlling are instantiated. No multiple twin instances or decentralized hierarchy are described; the architecture is centralized around one twin instance.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, mapping each Functional Entity to an individual ROS 2 package; each package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the FE functionality. Intra‑entity communication uses the ROS 2 publish/subscribe network, while inter‑entity communication is carried over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. A Data Translation FE serialises all streams, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Services are composed of SW components (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, ACC Controlling, Reporting) that are orchestrated by the DT Entity and invoked by the User Interface FE.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: External integration is realised through ROS 2 middleware for internal data exchange and UDP over Wi‑Fi for communication between the physical twin and the DT. The Data Translation FE may be implemented with OPC UA (IEC 62541) to provide standardized data‑exchange semantics. The system therefore inter‑operates with external devices and enterprise tools via OPC UA‑compatible interfaces and standard network protocols (ROS 2, UDP).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin System comprises several computational models: (1) a behavioral Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) model that determines the lead vehicle from sensor inputs and computes a desired speed based on inter‑vehicle distance; (2) a geometric point‑cloud clustering and tracking model that processes raw LiDAR point clouds into object tracks; (3) a Data‑Translation model that serializes all data streams, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Data sources are the LiDAR sensor and vehicle‑state sensors (speed, position). Model relationships: the point‑cloud model supplies object‑track data to the ACC model; the ACC model outputs desired speed and brake commands to the actuation FE. Data management is realized through ROS 2 nodes organized as Functional Entities (Data Collection FE, Data Storage FE, Reporting FE, Presentation FE). Intra‑entity communication uses the ROS 2 publish/subscribe mechanism; inter‑entity communication uses a Wi‑Fi UDP transport layer. The Data Storage FE persists raw sensor data, processed tracks and ACC reports for later analysis.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The Physical Twin Entity hosts two actuation Functional Entities: Motor Actuation FE that receives speed commands and drives the vehicle’s drive motor, and Steering Servo Actuation FE that receives steering angle commands to steer the vehicle. Control commands are generated by the ACC Controlling FE (within the Digital Twin Entity) and by the Remote Management UI (enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, emergency braking). Commands are transmitted over the ROS 2 network to the respective actuation FEs and finally over the Wi‑Fi UDP link to the on‑board hardware. The scale 1/10 autonomous vehicle limits actuation ranges to those of the miniature platform. Safety‑critical operation requires that command timestamps be synchronized within 100 ms, as dictated by the hardware limitation for clock synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing stack consists of a LiDAR sensor that produces raw point‑cloud data and a set of vehicle‑state sensors (speed, position, motor status) providing the current vehicle state. LiDAR data is consumed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking FE for preprocessing and object‑track generation. Vehicle‑state data is fed directly to the ACC model. All sensor streams are published as ROS 2 topics and subsequently relayed to the Digital Twin Entity via the Wi‑Fi UDP transport. The documents do not specify sampling frequencies, resolution, or transmission rates, but indicate that the system must maintain clock synchronization within 100 ms for safety‑critical use.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by maintaining real‑time synchronization of all components within a 100 ms bound, as required for safety‑critical vehicular applications. The ACC behavioral model’s output is validated visually on the 3D Visualization UI and quantitatively through the Reporting FE, which logs desired speed versus actual vehicle response. The point‑cloud clustering & tracking model’s accuracy is supported by the Data Translation FE that enforces consistent units and message formats, reducing data‑type errors. Uncertainty sources include sensor noise from LiDAR and vehicle‑state measurements; these are mitigated by the preprocessing FE that filters and clusters raw data before it is used by higher‑level models. Quality assurance is performed by the Data Storage FE that persists raw and processed data for post‑run analysis, and by the Reporting FE that generates reports for developers to assess model performance.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor streams (LiDAR point cloud, vehicle speed, steering angle) generated by the 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle are transmitted to the Digital Twin System (DTS) over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. Within the DTS, intra‑entity communication among software components follows a ROS 2 network (DDS). The raw LiDAR point cloud is first consumed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component (implemented as the Data Pre‑Processing Functional Entity on the Physical Twin Entity), which clusters points using DBSCAN and tracks them with a Kalman filter. Vehicle speed and steering angle are captured by the Data Collection component (Data Collecting FE). All data streams then pass through the cross‑system Data Translation FE, which serializes the messages, aligns their types, and performs unit conversions before they are stored or visualized. Data are continuously collected in real‑time (no explicit sampling period given) to support monitoring and further processing.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Commands originating from the Digital Twin Entity (e.g., ACC‑derived desired speed, enable/disable ACC, emergency‑brake request) are issued by the Control Panel (User Interface FE) and forwarded through the Data Translation FE, then sent over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link to the Physical Twin Entity. The Actuation Functional Entity on the Physical Twin receives the desired speed command, converts it into motor voltage signals, and drives the vehicle actuators; enable/disable and emergency‑brake commands directly trigger the Actuation FE to stop or modify actuation. Command messages are serialized and unit‑converted by Data Translation, providing basic format validation; the system relies on the ROS 2/DDS infrastructure for reliable delivery, but no explicit authentication or conflict‑resolution mechanisms are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS employs a hybrid network architecture: intra‑entity software components communicate via a ROS 2 network (DDS) on each host, while inter‑entity traffic between the Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User entities uses a Wi‑Fi network with UDP transport. Clock synchronization among devices is required with ≤ 100 ms accuracy to support safety‑critical off‑loading. The network is designed for low end‑to‑end latency to meet real‑time monitoring and remote‑management requirements. No explicit bandwidth figures are given; reliability is based on ROS 2 QoS settings and UDP, and security protocols (e.g., encryption or authentication) are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The DTS is deployed across both edge (on‑board vehicle hardware) and external compute platforms. The Data Pre‑Processing FE (LiDAR clustering & tracking) and the Actuation FE reside on the vehicle (edge) as ROS 2 packages, providing immediate access to sensors and actuators. Higher‑level services—Visualization FE, Data Collection FE, Reporting FE, ACC Controlling FE, and the User Interface FE—run on an external host (e.g., cloud or edge server) that offers greater processing capacity. Collected vehicle state and object tracks are persisted in an external SQL database (Data Storage). The architecture leverages modular ROS 2 nodes, enabling scalability by adding or replicating services, and supports deployment models ranging from single‑machine to distributed cloud‑edge configurations. Availability is addressed by separating critical actuation (on‑vehicle) from non‑critical analytics (off‑board), though explicit redundancy or failover mechanisms are not described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The automotive digital twin is implemented in compliance with ISO 23247 (Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework), specifically Part 2’s reference architecture, employing the entity‑based model (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) and mapping each Functional Entity to a ROS 2 package. The design references IEC 62541 (OPC UA) for the Data Translation functional entity and adheres to a ≤ 100 ms clock‑synchronisation requirement for safety‑critical vehicular use. Safety considerations are linked to ISO 26262, and the architecture is aligned with AUTOSAR principles to enable hardware‑agnostic component deployment. The prototype is released as open‑source code via the McMaster Centre for Software Certification, supporting interoperability with existing automotive standards.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin System (DTS) follows the ACC control strategy defined in ISO 15622 (performance class I, no road‑curvature capability) as required by requirement R6, thereby grounding the safety‑critical control loop in an established automotive functional‑safety specification. The case‑study discussion (Document 2) stresses that safety must be promoted to a first‑class citizen in automotive DT standards and recommends alignment with ISO 26262 functional‑safety requirements for any safety‑critical DT functions. Security concerns are acknowledged in the same study, which calls for dedicated security research and highlights the relevance of blockchain‑based e/e‑traceability for data integrity as described by Heber et al. (Document 3). No specific cybersecurity protocols, access‑control mechanisms, or authentication schemes are detailed in the provided documents.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements

_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.

Although the standard was developed for the manufacturing
domain, its RA is an appealing feature for DT developers in
other domains. Shtofenmakher and Shao [16] adapt the RA
of ISO 23247 to space debris detection. In their approach,
the monolithic User Interface FE is decomposed into four
distinct FEs, each providing a specific viewpoint for space
data systems, public users, and satellite owners and operators,
who need to interact with the DT in very different ways. Since
not all space objects are controllable, the OME is decomposed
into controllable and uncontrollable Resident Space Objects.
Both types of OMEs are able to provide data through the
Data Collection and Control Entity. Cederbladh et al. [17]
investigate the fitness of the RA for DTs of heterogeneous
battery systems in electric vehicles. In their proposal, the DT
Entity is split into two components to execute on separate
hardware. A lightweight real-time component deals with timesensitive computation and an offline component deals with
computation-intensive tasks. Liu and David [15] investigate the
fitness of the RA for AI simulation, the technique of training
AI agents through simulated data. The proliferation of DTs has
had a positive impact on simulator technology too: today, some
of the most advanced simulators are found in DTs, making an
appealing case for implementing AI simulation by DTs. In
addition, DTs can collect data from the physical twin through
purposeful experimentation [18]. The _Collection Identification_
_FE_ is an apt spot to implement such features, while AI agents
can integrate with the DT through the User Entity.
These works underscore the utility of the ISO 23247 RA
outside manufacturing. Of course, part of this utility is due
to the lack of alternatives. Nonetheless, by properly mapping
domain-specific requirements, the RA can serve as a useful
starting point in the development of DTs outside of manufacturing. Our work is the first attempt at investigating the fitness
of the ISO 23247 RA for automotive systems.


_B. Automotive digital twins_

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections [1] of real assets [2], offering cost-efficient
and safe alternatives for interacting with the asset and controlling it [3]. Digital twinning has been a transformative paradigm
in the engineering of complex cyber-physical systems [4],
many of which are safety-critical, such as manufacturing
systems [5], power grids [6], and biophysical systems [7]. The
success of DTs in such safety-critical domains made the case
for DTs in the automotive industry too [8], marked by an
increased interest in automotive DTs [9].
The complexity of DTs warrants careful architectural design. Unfortunately, the relative scarcity of architectural standards and reference architectures (RAs) for DTs hinder their
design, development, and by extension, their adoption. This
is a particularly acute problem in heavily standardized domains, such as automotive. Currently, the only DT-specific
architectural standard is the relatively new ISO 23247 –
Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework [10]. Published in
2021 by the International Organization for Standardization
(ISO), the standard defines a development framework for
digital twins, particularly in the manufacturing domain. Part 2
of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA designed and
developed for manufacturing systems. The other few available
DT standards are rather high-level and conceptual, and do
not define a RA—e.g., ISO/IEC 30173:2023 (Digital twin
— Concepts and terminology) [11]. As a consequence, DTs

outside manufacturing are currently architected through standards that offer solutions to sub-problems of DT engineering.
Such limitations have been reported in an array of domains,
e.g., industrial IoT [12] and systems of systems [13]. In the
absence of better-suited alternatives, the ISO 23247 RA has
drawn increasing interest, e.g., in edge computing [14] and
AI simulation [15], and its adaptation for some domains has
already begun, e.g., in aerospace [16] and battery systems [17].
In this paper, we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247
RA for automotive DTs. Through a case study of a DT for
the adaptive cruise control (ACC) system in a 1/10 [th] -scale
autonomous vehicle, we identify benefits and limitations of
the RA, and open a discussion about standardized automotive
DT architectures. To facilitate reuse, we make our research
artifacts (source code and documentation) publicly available. [1]

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

_B. Automotive digital twins_


In the automotive context, the _ego vehicle_ refers to the
vehicle of interest hosting hardware and software that enable
autonomous driving. Streams of data from LiDAR, cameras,
and other sensors are fused in real time to detect objects in the
ego vehicle’s environment. Each object is typically assigned
an ID and is characterized by its state estimate—e.g., size,
position, velocity—which is updated every perception cycle,
and is called an _object track_ . Using these tracks, ACC can
be developed, which is an advanced driver assistance system
(ADAS) that adjusts the ego vehicle’s speed to maintain a
safe following distance behind the vehicle in its driving path,
called the _lead vehicle_ . The driver can set a _desired speed_ to
be maintained when no lead vehicle is present.
DTs can also be used to streamline the development of
vehicles and ADAS systems, or improve their operation [19].
During the design phase of automotive systems, DTs are often
used as cyber-physical experimentation facilities, thanks to
their advanced simulator components and the tight coupling



between the virtual and physical environments [20]. This is
often achieved in combination with 3D virtual technology, e.g.,
3D CAD design [21]. DTs are also used to validate compliance
with requirements [22]. During vehicle operation, DTs are
used for a variety of optimization and health monitoring
purposes. Ramtilak et al. [23] use a DT of spark ignition
to improve fuel economy and driving performance. Magargle
et al. [24] use DTs to collect vehicle data and analyze
vehicle performance to inform drivers. Rajesh et al. [25]
implement predictive maintenance of a brake system using a
DT, integrating a state-of-the-art simulator, CREO Simulate,
into it. Venkatesan et al. [26] implement a DT for health
monitoring and prognosis of a permanent magnet synchronous
motor. DTs are also used as a product lifecycle management
tool in support of digital threads of vehicles, establishing
a continuous, end-to-end information flow throughout the
lifecycle [27]. Additional insights into the role and applications
of DTs in automotive are available in the surveys of Deng et al.

[9] and Schwarz and Wang [28].

the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations

the monolithic User Interface FE is decomposed into four

data systems, public users, and satellite owners and operators,
who need to interact with the DT in very different ways. Since
not all space objects are controllable, the OME is decomposed
into controllable and uncontrollable Resident Space Objects.

battery systems in electric vehicles. In their proposal, the DT

hardware. A lightweight real-time component deals with timesensitive computation and an offline component deals with
computation-intensive tasks. Liu and David [15] investigate the
fitness of the RA for AI simulation, the technique of training
AI agents through simulated data. The proliferation of DTs has
had a positive impact on simulator technology too: today, some
of the most advanced simulators are found in DTs, making an
appealing case for implementing AI simulation by DTs. In
addition, DTs can collect data from the physical twin through
purposeful experimentation [18]. The _Collection Identification_
_FE_ is an apt spot to implement such features, while AI agents
can integrate with the DT through the User Entity.
These works underscore the utility of the ISO 23247 RA
outside manufacturing. Of course, part of this utility is due
to the lack of alternatives. Nonetheless, by properly mapping
domain-specific requirements, the RA can serve as a useful
starting point in the development of DTs outside of manufacturing. Our work is the first attempt at investigating the fitness

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections [1] of real assets [2], offering cost-efficient
and safe alternatives for interacting with the asset and controlling it [3]. Digital twinning has been a transformative paradigm
in the engineering of complex cyber-physical systems [4],
many of which are safety-critical, such as manufacturing
systems [5], power grids [6], and biophysical systems [7]. The
success of DTs in such safety-critical domains made the case
for DTs in the automotive industry too [8], marked by an
increased interest in automotive DTs [9].
The complexity of DTs warrants careful architectural design. Unfortunately, the relative scarcity of architectural standards and reference architectures (RAs) for DTs hinder their
design, development, and by extension, their adoption. This
is a particularly acute problem in heavily standardized domains, such as automotive. Currently, the only DT-specific
architectural standard is the relatively new ISO 23247 –
Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework [10]. Published in
2021 by the International Organization for Standardization
(ISO), the standard defines a development framework for
digital twins, particularly in the manufacturing domain. Part 2
of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA designed and
developed for manufacturing systems. The other few available
DT standards are rather high-level and conceptual, and do
not define a RA—e.g., ISO/IEC 30173:2023 (Digital twin
— Concepts and terminology) [11]. As a consequence, DTs

outside manufacturing are currently architected through standards that offer solutions to sub-problems of DT engineering.
Such limitations have been reported in an array of domains,
e.g., industrial IoT [12] and systems of systems [13]. In the
absence of better-suited alternatives, the ISO 23247 RA has
drawn increasing interest, e.g., in edge computing [14] and
AI simulation [15], and its adaptation for some domains has
already begun, e.g., in aerospace [16] and battery systems [17].
In this paper, we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247
RA for automotive DTs. Through a case study of a DT for
the adaptive cruise control (ACC) system in a 1/10 [th] -scale
autonomous vehicle, we identify benefits and limitations of
the RA, and open a discussion about standardized automotive
DT architectures. To facilitate reuse, we make our research
artifacts (source code and documentation) publicly available. [1]

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of

**R4. The DTS shall report real-time ego vehicle informa-**
**tion through a UI.** Drivers are more likely to trust ADAS
systems with transparent human-machine interfaces that
report relevant information of the ADAS decision-making
process [34]. Thus, the UI shall display the following
information about the ego vehicle in real-time:

   - ego vehicle’s speed;

   - ego vehicle’s steering angle;

   - tracks surrounding the ego vehicle;

   - projected driving path of the ego vehicle.


**R5. The DTS shall include a user interface to remotely**
**manage the ACC sub-system.** To enable remote management, the UI implements the following commands:

   - enable and disable ACC;

   - modify the desired speed of ACC;

   - perform emergency braking of the ego vehicle.


**R6. The DTS shall actuate the ego vehicle in response**
**to user commands and ACC control.** The system
shall implement ACC, as outlined in ISO 15622 [35].
The basic control strategy and functionality, described in

[35, Sec 6.1–6.2] shall be implemented for performance
class I ACC (no road curvature capabilities), while the
remaining requirements are not considered due to ACC
being implemented on a 1/10 [th] -scale vehicle.
